The province of Assam lies on the north-eastern border of Bengal, with Bhutan and Tibet beyond it on the north, and Burma and Manipur on the east. It comprises the valleys of the Brahmaputra and the Surma, together with the mountainous watershed which intervenes between these two rivers. It is situated between 24^{\circ} 0' and 28^{\circ} 17' N. lat., and between 89^{\circ} 46' and 97^{\circ} 5' E. long. The total area is 56,243 square miles, inclusive of South Lushai and Manipur; the total population, including the hill tracts, amounts to 6,126,343, according to the census of 1901. The administrative headquarters, and

residence of the chief commissioner, are at the station of Shillong, in the Khasi Hills.

Assam is bounded on the north by the eastern section of the great Himalayan range, the frontier tribes from west to east being successively Bhutias, Akas, Dafas, Miris, Abors, and Mishmis; on the north-east by the Mishmi Hills, which sweep round the head of the Brahmaputra valley; on the east by the unexplored mountains that mark the frontier of Burma, by the hills occupied by independent Naga tribes, and by the state of Manipur; on the south by the hills occupied by the Kukis or Lushais, by the state of Hill Tippera, and by the Bengal district of Tippera; on the west by the Bengal districts of Maimansingh and Rangpur, the state of Kuch Behar, and Jailpaigur district.

The province of Assam was constituted in the year 1874, when the eleven districts comprising it were separated from the lieutenant-governorship of Bengal, and erected into an independent administration under a chief commissioner. The tract thus united under one government is naturally divided into three portions,—the Brahmaputra valley, the Surma valley, and the intervening hill tracts,—each of which in former times possessed a separate history. To the Brahmaputra valley alone, covering an area of 20,860 square miles, or nearly one-half the whole, is the name of Assam properly applicable. This fertile valley, penetrated throughout its entire length by a great navigable river, has always been closely connected with the main course of Indian history; though it has never formed an integral part of the vast empires which dominated at various times over the rest of the peninsula. In ethnological features and in language, as well as in history, the population of Assam are distinct from the great body of Hindus, though in recent times they have adopted the Brahmanical religion. The earliest authentic traditions attest the existence of a Hindu kingdom of Kamrup, with its capital at Gauhati, still the most important town of the province. The area of this kingdom is said to have extended over a great part of Eastern Bengal, including the present district of Rangpur.

The three divisions of Assam are distinguished by well-marked physical features. Assam Proper, or the valley of the Brahmaputra, is an alluvial plain, about 450 miles long, with an average breadth of 50 miles. The Surma valley reproduces the same phenomena on a smaller scale. The district of Cachar is crossed by hill ranges running transversely to the main river; and, in Sylhet, the valley gradually expands until it merges into the wide expanse of Eastern Bengal. The central hill tract, comprising the districts of the Naga Hills, Khasi and Jaintia Hills and Garo Hills, forms a long, projecting outwork of the mountain system that intervenes between the watersheds of the Brahmaputra and the Irawadi.

The soil of the Brahmaputra valley is, for the most part, a rich, black loam, reposing on grey sandy clay, but in some tracts a light yellow clay appears on the surface. The mountains on the north and east are of igneous formation, being composed of primitive limestone, granite, serpentine, porphyry, and talcose slate. The hills of Assam abound in mineral resources, including coal, iron, and limestone.

Regarded ethnically, the population of Assam presents a great variety of races. As compared

with Bengal, tribes of aboriginal or semi-aboriginal descent are especially numerous.

The population of Assam is entirely rural. All alike depend for their livelihood upon agriculture. Not a single town contains a large commercial or industrial element.

The staple crop in all parts of Assam is rice. In the valley of the Brahmaputra the greater part of the total cultivated area is devoted to the growth of that cereal. The other crops include mustard-seed in abundance, chiefly sown on marshy tracts, pulses to a small extent, sugar-cane, Indian corn, betel-nut and betel-leaf, and tobacco. The cultivation of jute is confined to the Sylhet and Goalpara districts, and that of cotton to the hill tracts. The soil of Assam is extremely fertile. Neither manure nor irrigation is required, as the rivers rarely fail to overflow their banks, and deposit a fresh top-dressing of silt every year. There is abundance of waste land on all sides waiting for tillage. The pressure of the population is at present very light. The revenue demand is comparatively low, and is levied in a manner most convenient to the cultivators.

The cultivation and manufacture of tea, conducted with European capital and under European supervision, form the great commercial industry of Assam. The tea-gardens occupy only a very small area, but they are the principal source of wealth to the province, and supply the chief stimulants to its development. India-rubber and lac are also valuable products; while timber from the extensive forests of the province is in considerable demand.

The principal native industry in Assam is the weaving of coarse silk cloth. Cotton cloth, brass-ware, pottery, ivory ornaments, and the ordinary utensils are also manufactured; but the indigenous manufactures of Assam have suffered greatly from the competition of the cheaper articles imported from Bengal. In Sylhet and the Surma Valley, there are specialities of sitalpati or grass mats, shell bracelets, ivory mats and fans, and inlaid iron-ware.

The external commerce is almost entirely conducted by water, though a railway from Chittagong to Dibrugarh is now in course of construction, and has been opened as far as Damchara. Both the Brahmaputra and Surma are navigable by steamers.

The administration of the province is entrusted to a chief commissioner, acting immediately under the orders of the government of India. Under him are the commissioner of the Assam Valley, and the political agent for Manipur State; next are eleven deputy-commissioners—one for each district—who conduct the various departments of the fiscal, executive, and, in some cases, judicial administrations. These posts are filled in accordance with the non-regulation system, being open to military officers and uncovenanted civilians, with the exception of Sylhet, which is reserved for a covenanted servant. There are two judges—one for Assam Proper, and the other for the two districts of the Surma Valley. The heads of departments include an inspector-general of police, of jails, of registration, and superintendent of stamps, commissioners of excise, an inspector of schools, a conservator of forests, a superintending engineer of public works, &c.

UNITED PROVINCES OF AGRA AND OUDH.

(Formerly known as the 'North-Western Provinces and Oudh.')

Lieutenant-governorship and chief-commissionership of British India, lying between 23^{\circ} 52' and 31^{\circ} 7' N. lat., and between 77^{\circ} 5' and 84^{\circ} 40' E. long. Area, Agra, 83,333 square miles; Oudh, 23,966 square miles; total, 107,164 square miles. Population in 1901, Agra, 34,858,705 persons; Oudh, 12,833,077; total, 47,691,782. Including native states, these totals become 112,243 square miles, and 48,493,879 persons. The provinces are bounded on the north by Tibet; on the northeast by Nepal; on the east by Bengal; on the south by Chutia Nagpur, Rewah, the Bundelkhand states, and the Central Provinces; and on the west by Gwalior, Rajputana, and the Punjab.

The United Provinces of Agra and Oudh occupy, roughly speaking, the upper basin of the Ganges and the Jumna, corresponding to Hindustan Proper of the Muhammadan chroniclers. A large semi-circular tract, comprising the valleys of the Gogra and the Gumti, has long been artificially separated from the remainder of the great plain, as the kingdom of Oudh; and though now under the administrative charge of the lieutenant-governor at Allahabad, it remains, in respect of its courts, a distinct chief commissionership. With this exception, the Province of Agra includes the whole upper portion of the wide Gangetic basin, from the Himalayas and the Punjab plain to the Vindhyan plateau, and the low-lying rice fields of Behar. Taken as a whole, the lieutenant-governorship consists of the richest wheat-bearing country in India, irrigated both naturally by the rivers which take their rise in the northern mountains, and artificially by the magnificent system of canals and distributaries, which owe their origin to British enterprise. It is studded with villages, interspersed at greater distances with commercial towns. Except during the hot-weather months, when the crops are off the fields, the general aspect in normal years is that of a verdant and well-tilled but very monotonous plain, only merging into hilly or mountainous country at the extreme edges of the basin on the south and north. The course of the great rivers marks the prevailing slope of the land, which falls away from the Himalayas, the Rajputana uplands, and the Vindhyan plateau south-eastwards towards the Bay of Bengal. The chief natural features of the provinces are thus determined by the main streams, whose alluvial deposits first formed the central portion of the United Provinces; while the currents have afterwards cut for themselves deep channels through the detritus brought down by their own agency from the ring of hills or uplands on every quarter.

The great mass of the people are still Hindus, although the followers of Islam had long been established as the dominant race.

Most of the people are gathered into small villages. There are, however, including Oudh, no fewer than 317 towns with a population exceeding 5,000 souls; and 16 with a population exceeding 50,000. No other part of India con-

tains so large a proportion of cities famous in Indian history, though Delhi, the most famous of all, has been made over to the adjoining Punjab province.

Out of a total area of 107,164 square miles, over 58,000 square miles are under cultivation. No part of India bears finer or more luxuriant crops than these provinces; and the natural fertility has been much increased by a magnificent series of irrigation works. The course of tillage comprises two principal harvests—the khariṛ, or autumn crops, sown in June, and reaped in October or November; and the rabi, or spring crops, sown in October or November, and reaped in March or April. The great agricultural staple is wheat, but millets and rice are also largely cultivated. The chief commercial crops include indigo, cotton, sugar, opium, oil-seeds, and tea. Rice and sugar-cane grow chiefly in the river valleys, or in irrigated fields; wheat is raised on the uplands by the aid of canals and wells; millets and cotton grow on the drier soils, while tobacco, vegetables, and other richer crops occupy the manured plots in the neighbourhood of the villages. The system of land tenure is based upon the ancient Aryan communal type, with various modifications from the purest form of joint-village proprietorship down to the separate ownership of particular plots.

The cultivation and manufacture of tea is confined to the submontane districts of Kumaun, Garhwal, and Dehra Dun.

The export trade is chiefly confined to the raw produce of its agriculture. The principal staples include wheat, oil-seeds, raw cotton, indigo, sugar, molasses, timber, and forest produce, dye-stuffs, ghi, opium, and tobacco. The imports consist mainly of English piece-goods, metal-work, manufactured wares, salt, and European goods. The chief centres of trade are Cawnpore, Allahabad, Mirzapur, Benares, Meerut, Koil, Hathias, Muttra, Agra, Farrukhabad, Moradabad, Chandausi, Bareli, Saharanpur, Ghaziabad, Kasganj, Bijnaur, Nagina, Najibabad, Gorakhpur, Ghazipur, Pilibhit, and Shahjahanpur.

The principal manufactures are those of sugar, indigo, and coarse cotton cloth. Ornamental metal-work is made at Benares. Among the factories on the English model are the Elgin and Muir Cotton Mills of Cawnpore, the Shahjahanpur rum manufacture, and the breweries at Mussooree and Naini Tal. The provinces contain little or no mineral wealth, the quarries being almost entirely confined to the supply of building stone, and of nodulated limestone (kankar) for road metal.

The great water-ways of the Ganges and the Jumna formerly afforded the principal outlet for the overflowing produce of the provinces, and they still carry off a large portion of the heavy traffic. The Gogra forms the main channel for the grain and cotton of Gorakhpur, Basti, and Azamgarh, and for the forest products of Nepal. But a network of railways has, to a large extent, superseded the navigable rivers as a means of communication. The Ganges, Lower Ganges, and Eastern Jumna canals are also navigable throughout their whole course.

The United Provinces of Agra and Oudh are under the administrative charge of a lieutenant-governor, who resides at Allahabad.

He has a legislative council established on the model of the Madras, Bombay and Bengal councils, in 1886, and enlarged to 15 members under the Indian Councils Act of 1892. For the purposes of this council he is lieutenant-governor of the United Provinces of Agra and Oudh.

PUNJAB.

Province of British India, under the administration of a lieutenant-governor with a legislative council; lying between 27° 39' and 35° 2' N. lat., and between 69° 35' and 78° 35' E. long. Area under direct British administration, 97,209 square miles; population, according to the census of 1901, 20,330,339. The native states in dependence upon the lieutenant-governor of the Punjab have an area of 35,532 square miles, with a population of 4,424,398 persons. The total area of the province, accordingly, amounts to 133,741 square miles, and its gross population (1901) to 24,754,737.* The Punjab is bounded on the north by Kashmir, and the hill states of Swat and Boner; on the east by the river Jumna, the United Provinces, and Tibet; on the south by Sind, the river Sutlej, and Rajputana; and on the west by the river Indus and Khelat. The capital of the Punjab is Lahore, situated about the centre of the province, but the principal city in population and importance is Delhi, the ancient metropolis of the Mughal dynasty.

In its strict etymological sense, the Punjab, or region of the Five Rivers, comprises only the tract of country enclosed and watered by the confluent streams of the Sutlej, the Beas, the Ravi, the Chenab, and the Jhelum. But modern territorial arrangements have included under the same designation, three other well-demarcated tracts, namely—the Sind Sagar Doab, or wedge of land between the Punjab Proper and the Indus; the Derajat, or narrow strip of country west of the Indus, and stretching up to the Sulaiman Mountains; and the cis-Sutlej Districts, or table-land of Sirhind, between the Punjab Proper and the Jumna, the greater part of which belongs, historically and physically, to the United Provinces; though now transferred for administrative purposes to the lieutenant-governor at Lahore. The districts of Ludhiana, Umballa and Firozpur, were placed under the resident at Lahore after the first Sikh war (1845-46); those of Delhi, Gurgaon, Rohtak, Karnal, Sirsa, and Hissar, under the Punjab government after the Mutiny. The political relations of the Sikh states were placed under the Punjab government as soon as it was constituted, and those of the petty Muhammadan states of Pataudi, Loharu, and Dujana, after the close of the events of 1857. The province also includes the isolated Himalayan valleys of Kangra, Kulu, Lahul, and Spiti.

The Punjab plain belongs naturally to the same wide and level table-land as the desert of Rajputana and the thirsty province of Sind. Suffering

* In 1901, certain districts of the Punjab were transferred to the newly-formed North-West Frontier Province.

like the rest of Northern India from a scanty and precarious rainfall, it would present a similar expanse of barren sand to that which stretches along its southern border, were it not for the fertilizing influence of the great rivers which take their rise among the snow-clad peaks of the Himalayas, and bring down a perennial supply of water during the driest seasons to the burnt and almost rainless country on either side.

Throughout the Punjab, except upon the hills, wood is scarce. The uplands are generally covered only with grass, shrubs, or low jungle of mimosa. Clumps of trees, however, cluster around the village sites; and government has done much to encourage arboriculture, both by forest conservation, and by planting groves round cantonments and public buildings, or along roads and canals.

In 1866, a chief court, consisting of two judges—a barrister, and a civilian—was substituted for the judicial commissioner, and was constituted the final appellate authority in criminal and civil cases, with powers also of original criminal jurisdiction in cases where European British subjects are charged with serious offences, and of original civil jurisdiction in special cases. Since that date three other judges have been added to the court.

Among the Muhammadans, the Pathans are found chiefly in the north-western hill districts, where they form the bulk of the population. Of the Rajput Muhammadans, the Bhattis of the central divisions demand notice from their former dominant position; as do also the Ranghars of Delhi and Hissar, and the Janjuhas of Rawal Pindi. The Jats, who have embraced the faith of Islam where the Musalmans predominate, and elsewhere either retain their original creed or have become Sikhs, form the backbone of the cultivating community, except in the trans-Indus region. The Gujars are chiefly herdsmen on the dry central plateaux. The Ghakkars of Rawal Pindi compose the gentry of the hill country in that neighbourhood. A large number of Kashmiri immigrants are found as weavers in the larger towns.

Among the Hindus and Sikhs, Brahmins are usually found. The Rajputs inhabit the hills and plains east of the Ravi. The Banias compose the trading class in the eastern districts; but beyond the Ravi and in the southern divisions, the Aroras take their place. The Chamars comprise the lowest stratum of the population, only just emancipated from serfdom under British rule. The agricultural classes are returned, in the census of 1901, at about 11,225,000.

The tillage of the Punjab extends mainly along the foot of the boundary mountains, and stretches in long strips by the side of the great arterial rivers. The agricultural year is divisible into the rabi or spring harvest, and the kharif or autumn harvest. For the former the principal crop is wheat. Next come barley and gram. Peas and other pulses cover a small area, and tobacco and vegetables are grown on garden-plots. Tea cultivation is followed with success chiefly in Kangra. The principal kharif crops are rice, millets, Indian corn, pulses, cotton, and sugar cane. The methods of agriculture still retain their primitive simplicity, scarcely differing from those in use during the Vedic period.

Artificial irrigation is almost universal, except in the country immediately below the hills, and in the inundated tracts beside the great rivers. The Bari Doab, the Western Jumna, the Sirhind, and the Sutlej inundation canals supply water to a large area. Taking the province as a whole, it may be estimated that between one-fifth and one-sixth of the area is the property of government; while upwards of four-fifths belong to private owners. The greater part of the area belonging to government is, however, little better than an arid prairie, and could not profitably be brought under cultivation without the aid of extensive works of irrigation. Some of the more favourably-situated portions are preserved as forest or grazing lands, and others are held under lease from government for purposes of cultivation; but almost the entire cultivated area of the province is included in the lands of private owners.

These lands are held subject to the payment of land revenue to the state, or to grantees holding from the state; and their revenue in 1901-02 amounted to £1,661,392. In some cases, these assignments are of the nature of the release of the revenue of lands belonging to the assignees, but they have no necessary connection with proprietary right; and in the majority of instances, the grantees are merely entitled to receive the revenue payable to government, the amount of which is limited in the same way as if it were paid direct to government.

The great mass of the landed property in the Punjab is held by small proprietors, who cultivate their own land in whole or in part. The chief characteristic of the tenure generally is, that these proprietors are associated together in village communities, having to a greater or less extent joint interests.

In some cases, all the proprietors have an undivided interest in all the land belonging to the proprietary community,—in other words, all the land is in common. It is, however, much more common for the proprietors to have their own separate holdings in the estate; and this separation may extend so far that there is no land susceptible of separate appropriation which is not the separate property of an individual or family.

The great centres of trade in the Punjab are Multan, Lahore, Amritsar, Ambala, Delhi, and Peshawar. The channels of traffic fall into four great divisions. That on the north frontier comprises the trade with Kashmir, Ladakh, Yarkand, Chinese Tibet, and Central Asia generally. That on the west frontier includes the trade with Kabul and Tirah. By both these routes, the traffic inward consists of charas (an intoxicating preparation of Indian hemp), dyes of various kinds, goat's wool, raw silk, fruits and nuts, wood, furs and feathers, and shawl cloth; while indigo, grain, metals, salt, spices, tea, tobacco, Indian and European cotton cloth, hides and leather, form the chief items of return trade. As the Punjab is essentially an agricultural country, the exports consist chiefly of grain, cotton, salt, and other raw produce; while the imports comprise cloth, hardware, and other manufactured articles. The mineral wealth of the province is almost confined to its rich deposits of rock salt.

The railway system of the Punjab is a continuation of that which extends from Calcutta into the United Provinces, and it has

now been put into direct connection with the sea at Karachi (Kurrachee) in Sind. The East Indian Railway sends a branch across the Jumna at Delhi, whence the Rajputana State Railway runs southward through Delhi and Gurgaon districts into Rajputana, ultimately extended to Bombay. The North-Western Railway continues the main system through the Gangetic Doab, crossing the Jumna into this province from Saharanpur district, and runs via Ambala, Ludhiana, Jalandhar, and Amritsar to Lahore; and thence continues the line as far as Peshawar on the north-west frontier; while another branch unites Lahore and Multan with Bahawalpur, Sukkur (Sakhar), and Karachi. A large part of the heavy traffic is conveyed by country boats on the Five Rivers, and thence by the Indus to the sea. Excellent metalled roads also connect the main centres of trade and the district headquarters.

The province is divided into 13 divisions; namely, Delhi, Ambala, Jalandhar, Hoshiarpur, Kulu, Ferozepore, Sialkot, Jhelum, Amritsar, Lahore, Rawal Pindi, Multan, and Shahpur.

CENTRAL PROVINCES.

The name given to the territory under the administration of a chief commissioner, lying between 17^{\circ} 50' and 24^{\circ} 27' N. lat., and between 76^{\circ} and 85^{\circ} 15' E. long., nearly coincident with the old geographical division of Gondwana. The tract falls naturally into several distinct areas, marked out by their physical features, and in a great measure by geological structure. To the north extends the Vindhyan table-land (including the districts of Sagar and Damoh), which sheds its waters northwards into the valley of the Ganges. Throughout this region, the surface is formed by the deposits styled Vindhyan, except in the large tracts where the Vindhyan strata are concealed by the overflowing volcanic rocks of the great Deccan trap area. South of Sagar and Damoh, in the valley of the Narbada, come Mandla (which includes the upper course of the river before it debouches into the plains), Jabalpur, Narsinghpur, Hoshangabad, and a part of Nimar, the rest of which lies in the valley of the Tapti. This area chiefly consists of alluvial and tertiary deposits, with a narrow belt of older rocks along the southern side of the valley. Continuing southwards, the next cluster of districts comprises Betul, Chhindwara, Seoni, and Balaghat, which occupy the extensive highlands constituting the Satpura table-land, in great part formed of the Deccan traps resting upon crystalline rocks, or upon sandstone and other rocks of later date. These districts at their central plateaux attain a height of about 2,000 feet. Still farther to the south extends the great Nagpur plain, formed by the valleys of the Wardha and Wainganga, which comprises the districts of Nagpur, Wardha, Bhandara, and Chanda. This region has no great elevation. It rests principally on gneissose and trap rocks, the former predominating in Nagpur and Bhandara, the latter in Wardha, eastwards. Below the ghats lies the Chhatisgarh plain, a low expanse of red soil, containing the districts of Raipur and Bilaspur. In this division is also included the district of Sambalpur, a rugged and jungly country, composed of crystalline and metamor-

phic rocks. Sambalpur is not, however, part of Chhatisgarh proper, either geographically or historically. It was originally attached to the south-western frontier agency of Bengal, and lies principally in the valley of the Mahanadi. Last of all, to the extreme south, almost cut off by forests and wild semi-independent states, is a strip of territory, of varied geological structure, stretching along the left bank of the Godavari, and attached to Chanda District.

The Central Provinces and the connected native states have (1901) a population of 11,873,029, and cover together an area of 115,894 square miles, of which little more than one-third is under cultivation. Yet the forests are not so important as might have been expected. The greater part of the waste land is covered by scrub jungle, and produces but little valuable timber.

The large coal-fields which extend under various parts of the Central Provinces, and the excellence of the iron ores, gave rise to expectations which at present seem unlikely to be realised. For the most part, on analysis, the coal has proved of inferior quality. It contains neither sufficient fixed carbon for iron smelting, nor combustible volatile gases to such an amount as to adapt it for generating steam. At present the only important colliery is that at Warora.

The Satpura plateau, stretching east and west for nearly 600 miles, with the wheat fields of the Narbada valley on the one hand, and the rice lands of the Nagpur plain on the other, forms the true barrier between northern and southern India. In this natural fastness, the so-called aboriginal tribes have found refuge, retreating on either side before the waves of Aryan immigration which swept forward from the Deccan and from Hindustan.

The principal agricultural products of the Central Provinces are rice, wheat, and other food grains. Cotton is grown chiefly in Nagpur, Wardha, and Nimar, and these districts, with Raipur and Bilaspur, have also the largest area under oil-seeds. The cultivation of tobacco is almost confined to Raipur.

The only important manufactures consist of weaving, and working iron ore. The tissue work of Burhanpur, and the richly-embroidered wearing apparel produced in parts of Nagpur and Bhandara, command an extensive sale beyond the province; and the excellence of the ores smelted near Gadarwara deserves notice. The internal trade is conducted by means of markets and fairs, the latter of which, for the most part, had a religious origin, and still retain a religious character. The chief external trade is with Bombay westward. The principal imports consist of cotton piece-goods, hardware, salt, cocoa-nuts, European liquors, tobacco, etc.; and the principal exports are raw cotton, grain, ghi, oil-seeds, and Indian piece-goods. Next in importance is the trade with the North-Western Provinces and Calcutta, the main imports being sugar from Mirzapur, piece-goods, indigo, jute bags, European liquors, etc.; and the exports, cotton for the mills at Cawnpore, lac, iron, grain, etc. With the Central Indian States a considerable traffic exists; but with the Nizam's dominions and Berar, and other parts of India, the trade is comparatively small. The Malwa opium, which passes through the province for export to China, now goes through Nimar to Bombay by rail.

The want of good means of communication, especially important in a land-locked region, has greatly retarded the progress of the Central Provinces. After the rains, the larger rivers become navigable, but the rocky barriers which occur in their channels restrict the use of this mode of transit. The making of roads, which may be said to date from the establishment of the British power, is rendered difficult by the nature of the country. Nagpur forms the centre of the road system. From that city branch off—the northern road, to Seoni and Jabalpur; the eastern line, by Bhandara and Raipur, to Sambalpur; the north-western, to Chhindwara; and the southern and south-western, to Chanda or Wardha. But besides these roads, numerous ancient tracks wind over hills and across the rocky beds of streams, along which the Banjaras drive their long trains of pack-bullocks. The Great Indian Peninsula Railway enters the Central Provinces near Burhanpur, and runs along the valley of the Narbada, passing Hosangabad, Narsinghpur, and Jabalpur, till it emerges from the north-east corner of the province, near Balihri. Starting from Bhusawal, a tributary line connects Wardha and Nagpur with the main railway, supplying communication with the coal-fields of Warora. A further branch has been constructed from Nagpur to Bilaspur, running thence east and north-east to join the East Indian Railway at Asansol; another line unites Bilaspur with Katni.

Nearly every form of land tenure found in India exists in the Central Provinces. Besides the estates of feudatory and of non-feudatory chiefs, known as zamindaris, the succession to which follows the law of primogeniture, what is termed the malguzari tenure prevails most widely. The estate, whether the property of one or many owners, is managed by a single proprietor, and the land is chiefly held by cultivators whose rents are thrown into a common stock. The profits are divided, or the losses made up, in proportion to the respective shares of the different proprietors.

BURMA.

The eastern-most province of British India, under the administration of a lieut.-governor; bounded on the north and north-east by China, and tribes subordinate to China; on the north-west by Bengal, Assam, and the feudatory state of Manipur; and on the west and south by the sea. To the south-east lies the kingdom of Siam. The total area, including the Shan States, Chin Hills, and Karenni is about 236,738 square miles, and the population 10,490,624.

The portion of Asia in which Burma is situated slopes from the central mountains towards the south; and the province is watered by five great streams, viz., the Irawadi and the Kyendwen, which unite their courses at 21^{\circ} 50' N. lat., the Sittaung or Paunglaung (Pounloug), the Salwin, and the Myit-nge. The first two rivers have their sources somewhere in the northern chain of mountains in the interior, one head stream of the Irawadi probably coming from Thibet; the Salwin has its source farther to the east in Thibet; the Sittaung in the hills to the south-east of Mandalay; and the Myit-nge, a large affluent of the Irawadi, drains the

Shan States to the east of the capital. Except the last, which has a westerly direction, they all run in a southerly course to the Indian Ocean. The Irawadi and Salwin are large rivers, which, in the lower part of their course, overflow the flat country below their banks during the season of the rains, and higher up force their way through magnificent defiles. The former is navigable a considerable distance above Bhamo; but the latter is practically useless as a means of communication, owing to the frequent obstacles in its channel.

The northern portion of the province is, in the main, an upland territory, containing much rolling country, intersected by occasional hill ranges, and with a few isolated tracts of alluvial plain. Three chief ranges of hills traverse the southern portion from north to south, the Arakan Yoma, the Pegu Yoma, and the Paunglaung, of which the Tenasserim Hills, forming the boundary between our territory and Siam, may be regarded as a prolongation.

The country throughout the Delta is flat and uninteresting. Towards Prome, the valley of the Irawadi contracts, and the monotony of the plain is diversified by a wooded range of hills. The Salwin valley contains occasional harmonies of forest, crag, and mountain stream; but they bear the same relation to the wild sublimity of the Himalayas as the Trossachs to the Alps. On the other hand, the scenery in Tavoy and Mergui, and among the myriad islets which fringe the Tenasserim coast, is almost English in its verdure and repose. The forests of Burma abound in fine trees. Among these, teak holds a conspicuous place, some of the finest teak forests being in Pegu. Almost every description of timber known in India is produced in the forests, from which also an abundant supply is obtained of the varnish employed by the Shans and the Burmese in their manufacture of lacquered ware. Sticklac of an excellent quality is obtained in the woods, and rubber of late years has been largely exported.

Burma is rich in minerals. It produces gold, silver, copper, tin, lead, antimony, bismuth, amber, coal, petroleum, nitre, natron, salt, limestone, and marble, the jade or yw of the Chinese, sapphires, and other precious stones. Gold is found in small quantities at Shwe-gyin, in the sands of different rivers, and also towards the Shan territory on the eastern frontier, but the demand is very much greater than the native supply. There are silver mines near the Chinese frontier, but they have not been recently worked. The mountainous districts of the Shan territory contain almost all the other metals; but they are but scantily exported, and the copper and tin seen in Mandalay are mostly imported from China. Iron is found in several places, and is wrought especially at Poppa, near a mountain of that name to the eastward of the old capital Pagan, and also at Myedu, north-west of Mandalay; but, owing to ignorance and the want of proper methods, about 30 or 40 per cent. of the metal is lost in the process. Large deposits of rich magnetic oxide, as yet untouched, exist in the ridges east of the capital near the banks of the navigable river Myit-nge, and the same district contains lime, in great abundance, and of remarkable whiteness; while statuary marble, equal to the best Italian kind, is found about 15

miles north of Mandalay to the east of the Irawadi. Mines of amber are wrought, among other places, at Hukhong or Payendwen, near the sources of Kyen-dwen. Nitre, natron, and salt are found in various quarters. Sulphur also occurs in some places, as in the district of Sale-myo, and in the neighbourhood of the petroleum wells; but the quantity is comparatively small. Coal has been discovered in various places. Petroleum is found in Kyauk-hpyu, the Boronga Islands, Thayet-myo, and Ye-nangyaung; and this industry is rapidly developing. The precious stones produced are chiefly the sapphire and the ruby. They are found about 60 or 70 miles in a north-east direction from Mandalay, over an area of about 100 square miles.

In Lower Burma, agriculture is the main employment of the people, and it may be assumed that the production and distribution of rice occupies three-fifths of the whole population. Cotton, sesamum, and tobacco are also extensively grown; gardens and orchards are found near every village; but rice covers about six-sevenths of the total area under cultivation. The enormous foreign demand, and the large profits recently obtained, have greatly increased the cultivation of the cereal. The Burmese are content with a single annual crop, corresponding with the aman ropa of Bengal. It is sown in June, transplanted in September, and reaped about December or January. Their soil is lavish in its yield, requires little labour, and no artificial stimulus beyond the ash of the past year's stubble, which is burned down and worked into the land. Year after year, without a rest, the heavy rains and this primitive manure suffice to ensure an abundant harvest. The Irawadi valley and its delta furnish about three-fifths of the whole rice produce.

Upper Burma, though inferior in point of fertility to the low-lying tracts of Lower Burma, is far from unproductive. The chief crops are rice (of which the Burmese count 102 different sorts), maize, millet, wheat, various pulses, tobacco, cotton, sesamum, mustard, and indigo. The sugar-cane appears to have been long known to the Burmese; but, though the climate and soil are extremely favourable, it is not generally cultivated. A cheap and coarse sugar is obtained from the juice of the Palmyra palm, which abounds in the tract south of Mandalay. The cocoa nut and areca palms are not common. The tea-plant, which is indigenous, is cultivated in the hills by some of the mountain tribes at the distance of about five days' journey, and by others in still greater perfection at the distance of about ten days' journey from Mandalay. It seems, however, to be another plant, probably the Elaodendron persicum, which furnishes the principal ingredient in the hlapet, or pickled tea, that forms a favourite Burmese delicacy, and is an essential accompaniment to every social or ceremonial incident. Cotton is grown chiefly in the dry lands and climate of the upper districts. Indigo is indigenous, and is universally cultivated, but in a very rude manner; it is still more rudely manufactured, so as to be wholly unfit for exportation abroad.

The commercial prosperity of Lower Burma has more than kept pace with its rapidly-increasing population. Rangoon absorbs over 80 per cent. of the whole foreign trade of the province.

The chief articles exported are rice, timber, cutch, hides, petroleum, and precious stones. The chief imports are piece-goods, silk, cotton and wool, cotton twist, gunny-bags, betel-nuts, liquors, tobacco, iron, mill machinery, and sugar.

The main commercial industries are those connected with the rice and timber trade. The indigenous manufactures of the country produce little beyond what is required for home consumption. Silk, lacquered ware, and gold and silver work, are among the most justly-admired of Burmese manufactures. The characteristics of Burmese art are vigour and novelty of design, but want of delicacy and finish in execution.

One of the most urgent wants of Burma is land communication. There is abundance of waterway, however, throughout the delta all the year round, but the Sittang valley has no such advantages. The railway to Mandalay from Toungoo (the then terminus of the Sittang Valley line), for which a preliminary survey was sanctioned in November, 1886, was opened for traffic in March, 1889, and afterwards extended to Mogauung; while further branches have since been constructed to Myitkyina and Myingyan. Since the railway was opened the districts through which it runs have been the quietest in Upper Burma. The traffic on the line is already considerable. Large outlay has been incurred and still continues upon barracks, hospitals, roads, and necessary civil buildings.

NORTH-WEST FRONTIER PROVINCE.

On 9th November, 1901, this new province was constituted by the transference from the Punjab of the districts of Peshawar, Kohat, Bannu (except the Mianwali sub-division) and Dera Ismail Khan (except its cis-Indus tahsils), and by the addition of the five trans-border political charges of Dir, Swat, and Chitral (with headquarters at Malakand), Khaibar, Kurram, Tochi, and Wana, together with three tahsils (Mansahra, Abbottabad, and Haripur) of Hazara. The province has an area of 16,466 square miles, and a population (1901) of 2,125,480.

NATIVE STATES.

That the territories of the Native States of India occupy no small proportion of the entire Indian peninsula and contain over one-fifth of the whole population of the country will be seen from the statistical table printed at page 357. The names of the principal chiefs, and the salutes allowed to each will be found at pp. 176A, 176B.

Many of the feudatory states are governed almost independently by their own rulers, but they are all subject in a greater or less degree to supervision and guidance at the hands of the British Government. They may be divided into two main heads: (1) states holding direct relations with the governor-general in council; and (2) states holding relations primarily with the several local governments of India.

STATES UNDER THE DIRECT SUPERVISION OF THE GOVERNOR-GENERAL IN COUNCIL.

1. Hyderabad.—The Hyderabad State is roughly co-extensive with the Deccan or central plateau of Southern India, and takes its name from its capital city. The total area of the territory governed by the Nizam is 82,698 square miles,

containing a population of 11,141,142, about one-tenth being Muhammadans. The ruling dynasty is of Turkoman origin, the name of the first sovereign being Chin Killich Khan, also known as Asaf Jah, who was appointed to be Viceroy of the Deccan with the title of Nizam-ul-Mulk in the year 1713. During the dissensions in the empire subsequent to the death of Aurungzeb, Asaf Jah succeeded in asserting his own independence. The Assigned Districts of Berar were assigned to the British Government in 1853 to meet the cost, including arrears, of the Hyderabad Contingent, an auxiliary force of four cavalry regiments, four batteries of field artillery, and six regiments of infantry. The raison d'être of this force was primarily that it might be of use to the Nizam, but it is available also under treaty for general service with British troops when required. The surplus revenue derived from Berar, after paying for the cost of the administration, is paid to the Nizam's government. Revenue of Hyderabad, about £2,500,000.

2. Mysore.—The Mysore State is situated in Southern India, between 11^{\circ} 40' and 15^{\circ} N. lat., and between 74^{\circ} 40' and 78^{\circ} 30' E. long., and is surrounded on all sides by British territory. Its total area is 29,444 square miles, and it contains a population of 5,539,399. The inhabitants are almost exclusively Hindus, who constitute more than 94 per cent. of the whole population. In early times Mysore was the principal seat of the Jains, who have left many interesting memorials of their occupation. The state has always been under Hindu rulers, except during the short interval caused by the usurpation of power during the 18th century by Haidar Ali and his son, Tipu Sultan. After the death of the latter, at the capture of Seringapatam in 1799, the English restored a representative of the ancient line in the person of Krishna Raj, son of Chama Raj of Arakotara. His subsequent misrule, when admitted to power on attaining his majority, led to the resumption of the administrative control of the province by the British government in the year 1831. The government, however, continued to be carried on in the name of the native prince, and at his death an adopted son was recognised as successor. This chief, since dead, was duly installed on coming of age in 1881. The British chief commissioner thereupon handed over office to the native diwan, and a political resident was appointed to represent British interests. Revenue of Mysore, about £1,450,000.

3. Baroda.—This state includes all the territories of its Highness the Gaekwar. Its total area is 8,099 square miles, and it contains a population of 1,952,692 souls, the majority of whom (over 79 per cent.) are Hindus. The province of Gujarat was at one time included in the Mughal empire, but, in the early part of the seventeenth century, the Mahrattas, under the leadership of Damaji Gaekwar, and afterwards of his son, Pilaji, succeeded in wresting all power out of the hands of the Mughal officers. From that time Baroda has remained continuously under the sway of the Gaekwar family, who held it in the first instance under the Mahratta Peshwa subject to a tributary payment, but afterwards threw off their allegiance and became feudatory to the British government under the guarantee of a treaty executed in the year

1817. The British government is represented at the court of the Baroda State by a political officer, called the resident and agent to the governor-general. Revenue of Baroda, about £1,000,000.

4. Kashmir and Jammu.—This native state includes, in addition to the district of Kashmir proper, Jammu, Poonch, and the governorships of Ladakh and Gilgit. Subordinate to it are the petty chiefships of Chitral, Hunza, Nagar, &c. The area is estimated to be 80,900 square miles. The population, according to the census of 1901, amounts to 2,905,578. Kashmir became part of the Mughal empire in 1586 A.D., but it was afterwards subjugated both by the Afghans and the Sikhs. At the time of the Sikh war the state was under the government of Gulab Singh, a Sikh officer, who ruled as a feudatory of the Maharaja of the Punjab. After the victory of the British forces in 1846, the possession of the kingdom was confirmed to Gulab Singh, under certain specified conditions of alliance and subordinate co-operation, by a separate treaty executed at Amritsar in the same year. A political resident is permanently stationed in Kashmir. The ruling family is Hindu, but about three-quarters of the inhabitants of the country are Muhammadans, and chiefly of the Sunni sect. Revenue, £438,000.

5. Kalat.—The Khan of Kalat is head of the Baluchistan tribal chiefs whose territories are comprised under the following divisions:—Jhalawan, Sarawan, Makran, Las, and Kachh Gandava. These districts form what may be termed Kalati Baluchistan, and occupy an area of 86,511 square miles. The inhabitants of the country are either Brahuis or Baluchis, both being Muhammadans of the Sunni sect. The country is sparsely populated, the total number being about half a million persons only. It derives its chief importance from its position with regard to Afghanistan on the north-western frontier of British India. The Khan of Kalat is under a treaty obligation of subordinate co-operation with the British government. The governor-general's agent in Baluchistan conducts the relations between the government of India and the Khan, and exercises a general political supervision over the district. Revenue, about £26,000.

6. Rajputana Agency.—Rajputana is the name of a great territorial circle, situated between 23^{\circ} and 30^{\circ} N. lat., and between 69^{\circ} 30' and 78^{\circ} 15' E. long. Within these limits are twenty native states, having each its own autonomy and separate chief, together with the British district of Ajmere-Merwara. Of the Native States, 17 are Rajput, 2 are Jat (Bhartpur and Dholpur), and 1 only is Muhammadan (Tonk). Their combined area is 127,541 square miles, and they contain a population (1901) of 9,723,301. The chief administrative control of the British district is vested ex officio in the political officer, who holds the post of governor-general's agent for the supervision of the relations between the several native states of Rajputana and the government of India.

The following table gives a detailed statement of the estimated area and population (including forest tribes) and the approximate revenues of the states in the Rajputana Agency:—

7. Central India Agency.—Central India is the name given to the country occupied by the native states grouped together under the supervision of the political officer in charge of the Central India Agency. These states lie between 21° 24' and 26° 32' N. lat., and between 74° 0' and 83° 0' E. long. The British districts of Jhansi and Lalitpur divide the Agency into two main divisions—Native Bundelkhand and Baghelkhand lying to the east, and Central India proper to the west. The total area covered is 78,772 square miles, and the population (1901) amounts to 8,628,781 (in 1891, 10,318,812). The great majority of the people

are Hindus. The principal states are eight in number—Gwalior, Indore, Bhopal, Rewa, Dhar, Jaora, Datia, and Orchha, of which two, Bhopal and Jaora, are Muhammadan, and the rest are Hindu. Besides these there are a multitude of petty states held by their rulers under the immediate guarantee of the British government, but having feudal relations with one or other of the larger states. The total number of states amounts to 69, and the petty chiefships to 79—total, 148.

The list below gives the approximate size, population, and revenue of the eight principal states above detailed:—

STATES UNDER THE LOCAL GOVERNMENTS AND ADMINISTRATIONS.

1. Under the Government of Madras.—The Madras Presidency includes five native states covering an area of 9,969 square miles, with a population (1901) of 4,188,086 souls.

Of these states, Travancore and Cochin represent ancient Hindu dynasties. Pudukattai is the inheritance of a chieftain called Tondiman. Banganapalli and Sandur, two petty states, of which the first is ruled by a Nawab, lie in the centre of two British districts. The principal states are:—

Name. Area. Population,
1901.		 Approximate
Revenue.
Sq. m. £
Cochin ... .. 1,362 815,218 156,000
Pudukattai ... .. 1,101 380,440 66,000
Travancore ... .. 6,730 2,951,038 621,000

2. Under the Government of Bombay.—The native states in this presidency number about 370. Area, 65,761 square miles. Population (1901), 6,908,648. The list below gives details of the area, &c., of the more important states:—

Name. Area. Population,
1901.		 Approximate
Revenue.
Sq. m. £
Bhaunagar ... .. 2,860 412,664 268,000

Gondal ... .. 1,024 162,859 100,000

3. Under the Government of Bengal there are twenty-nine states. Area, 38,652 square miles. Population (1901), 3,748,544. Revenue, about £385,000.

These states may be divided into two groups: the first consisting of the petty states attached to the Orissa and Chota Nagpur divisions, 24 in number; and the other comprising the states of Kuch Behar to the north, and Hill Tipperah to the east, of Bengal. The inhabitants

of the first group are hillmen of Kolarian or Dravidian origin, and their condition is still very primitive. Kuch Behar is inhabited by a Mongoloid people, the Koch. The population of Sikkim consists of the Thibetan races of Lepcha and Bhóti, and the Nipáli tribe, Limbu. Hill Tipperah is the home of a Mongoloid race, partly drawn from the Eastern Himalayas, and allied to the Kachári of Assam. The principal states are:—

Name. Area. Population,
1901.		 Approximate
Revenue.
Sq. m. £
Kuch Behar ... .. 1,307 566,974 149,000
Hill Tipperah ... .. 4,086 173,325 42,000

4. Under the Government of the United Provinces of Agra and Oudh, the native territory comprises the following two states, the first

being ruled by a Muhammadan, and the second by a Hindu dynasty.

Name. Area. Population,
1901.		 Approximate
Revenue.
Sq. m. £
Rampur ... .. 945 532,067 212,000
Tehri (Garhwal) ... .. 4,164 267,608 21,000

Rampur is nearly equally divided between Muhammadans and Hindus. The inhabitants of Tehri are chiefly Hindus.

5. Under the Government of the Punjab there are 34 states, varying considerably in size and importance. Area, 36,532 square miles. Population (1901), 4,424,398. Revenue, about £1,000,000.

The Punjab states may be grouped under three main classes. The hill states, 23 in number, lie among the Punjab Himalayas and are held by some of the most ancient Rajput

families in all India. Along the western half of the southern border lies the Muhammadan state of Bahawalpur. The remaining states, including the Sikh principalities of Patiala, Jind, Nabha, Kapurthala, Faridkot and Kalsia, and the Muhammadan chiefships of Maler Kotla, Pataudi, Loharu and Dujana, lie east of Lahore, and, with insignificant exceptions, occupy the centre of the eastern plains of the province.

6. Under the Chief Commissioner of the Central Provinces are 15 states. Their total area is 29,435 square miles, with a population (1901) of

1,996,383 persons. Revenues, about £120,000. The inhabitants are chiefly Hindus and aborigines. The principal states are:—

7. Under the Chief Commissioner of Assam there is only one state—Manipur. Its area is 3,284* square miles, and it has a population (1901) of 284,465, of which about 60 per cent, are Hindus, 36 per cent. animistic forest tribes, and 4 per cent. Muhammadans. Revenue, about £26,000.

there are a number of Shan states and other petty chiefships, the population of which is 1,228,460. The Shan States are, properly speaking, not Native States, the territory being part of British India. They are administered however, through the Sawbwas or hereditary chiefs.

8. Under the Lieut.-Governor of Burma

The above table only includes languages which are spoken by upwards of 100,000 persons. There are a large number of languages and dialects, each spoken by less than that number, which have been omitted through considerations of space. Altogether more than 100 distinct languages are spoken in various parts of India.

LIST OF PARLIAMENTARY PAPERS RELATING TO INDIA ISSUED

DURING 1903

H.C. 383 of Sess., 1902. Opium : Correspondence.
H.C. 406 " 1902. Loans raised in India, half-year ended 30th Sept., 1902.
H.C. 95 " 1903. Net Income and Expenditure, 1891-92 to 1901-02.
H.C. 98 " 1903. Public Works Department, Officers of, Memorials from, Correspondence, &c.
H.C. 103 " 1903. Loans raised in England, half-year ended 31st March, 1903.
H.C. 137 " 1903. Railways and Irrigation Works : Return of Capital Expenditure, &c.
H.C. 151 " 1903. Financial Statement and Discussion thereon, 1903-04.
H.C. 156 " 1903. Estimate of Revenue and Expenditure, 1902-03.
H.C. 157 " 1903. Home Accounts, 1901-02.
H.C. 210 " 1903. Loans raised in India, half-year ended 31st March, 1903.
H.C. 220 " 1903. India Office (Retirement at 65).
H.C. 237 " 1903. Liability for Increase in British Soldiers' Pay : Correspondence.
H.C. 249 " 1903. Moral and Material Progress Report : Decennial, 1892-93 to 1901-02.
H.C.B. 106 " 1903. Contracts (India Office) Bill.
Cd. 1445 " 1903. Trade Tables, 1897-98 to 1901-02.
Cd. 1515 " 1903. Entertainment of Indian and Colonial Guests : Cost.
Cd. 1644 " 1903. Delhi Durbar, Account of.
Cd. 1677 " 1903. Explanatory Memorandum, Accounts and Estimates, 1903-04.
Cd. 1713 " 1903. Railways : Administration and Working, Robertson's Report.
Cd. 1718 " 1903. Railway Administration Report, 1902.
Cd. 1747 " 1903. Agricultural Banks, Co-operative Credit Societies, Report.
Cd. 1797 " 1903. Imperial Institute (Indian Section), Report, 1902-03.
Cd. 1801 " 1903. Statistical Abstract, 1892-93 to 1901-02.
Cd. 1802 " 1903. Trade Review, 1902-03.
Cd. 1850 " 1903. Sanitary Measures : Report, 1901-02.

NOTE.—Any of the above-mentioned Parliamentary Papers may be obtained on application to Messrs. HARRISON AND SONS, 59, Pall Mall, S.W., who will also forward particulars of price, &c., if desired.

LIST OF THE MORE IMPORTANT INDIAN GOVERNMENT PUBLICATIONS
ISSUED DURING 1903.

THE FOLLOWING HAVE BEEN ADDED TO THE STOCK OF BOOKS ON SALE AT THE INDIA OFFICE.

Archæological Survey of India:—

Vol. XXXII. Architectural Antiquities of Northern Gujarat. By J. Burgess, C.I.E., LL.D., and H. Cousins. (₹1 11s. 6d.)

Flora of the Upper Gangetic Plain. By J. F. Duthie, B.A. Vol. I., Part I. (9s.)

Catalogue of the Persian MSS. in the India Office Library. By H. Ethé, Ph.D. Vol. I. (₹3 3s.)

Catalogue of Two Collections of Sanskrit MSS. in the I. O. Library. By C. H. Tawney, C.I.E., M.A., and F. W. Thomas, M.A. (2s.)

Technical Art Series, 1902. (4s. 6d.)

Mundari Grammar. By Rev. J. Hoffmann. (6s.)

Finance and Revenue Accounts, 1901-02. (3s.)

IN ADDITION TO THE USUAL PROVINCIAL AND DEPARTMENTAL REPORTS, THE FOLLOWING WORKS OF GENERAL INTEREST HAVE BEEN PUBLISHED IN INDIA.

Epigraphia Indica. Vol. VI., Part 8. Vol. VII., Parts 4, 6, 7.

Geological Survey Report, 1902-1903.

Meteorological Memoirs. Vol. XIV. (4s. 6d.)

Weather Review of India, 1901. (1s. 6d.) 1902. (4s. 6d.)

Indian Trade Review, 1902-1903. (9d.)

Prices and Wages in India, 1903. (2s. 3d.)

Trade and Navigation: Annual Statement, 1901-1902, Part 2. (3s.) [Also Monthly Statements, 9d. each.]

Monthly Land Trade Accounts. (9d. each.)

Financial and Revenue Statistics, 1893-1901. (3s.)

Financial and Commercial Statistics, 1902-1903. (6s.)

Statistics of Mineral Production, 1893-1902. (3d.)

Statistics of Production of Tea in India, 1902.

Lists of Ancient Records in Madras, 1789-1791.

Lists of Ancient Records of Government of India, 1760-1764.

Lists of Bengal and Madras Papers in Imperial Record Office, 1746-1785.

Digest of Indian Law Cases. By J. V. Woodman. Vol. VI. (18s.)

Malaria in India. By Capt. S. P. James. (2s. 5d.)

Trees, Shrubs, &c., of Bombay Presidency. By W. A. Talbot. 2nd Edn. (6s.)

Rise of Bombay. By S. M. Edwardes. (6s.)

Pests and Blights of the Tea Plant. By Sir G. Watt and H. H. Mann. 2nd Edn. (4s. 6d.)

Seir Mutaqherin—a Translation. 4 vols.

Monographs on—

Wood Carving in Assam (1s. 2d.); in United Provinces. Tanning and Working in Leather in Bombay (2s. 8d.); Silk Fabrics of Bengal. (7s. 6d.)

THE FOLLOWING ARE THE PRINCIPAL MAPS ADDED TO THE SALE STOCK AT THE INDIA OFFICE.

Except where otherwise stated, the maps are on one sheet. The figures in italics denote the number of miles to the inch.)

General Maps.—India, 64 (9s.); Railway System of India, 80 (3s.); India (without hills), 128 (3s.).

District and Provincial Maps.—Lakhimpur, 12 (9d.); Maymyo, 4 (2s.); Central Provinces, 16 (8s. 3d.); Central Provinces and Berar, 32 (1s. 6d.); United Provinces, 32 (1s. 11d.); Assam, 16 (3s.); Ranchi, 4 (2s. 8d.); Upper Burma, 16 (5s. 3d.); Darrang, 8 (9d.); Ahmednagar, 4 (3s. 3d.); Central India Agency, 80 (9d.).

Indian Atlas Sheets.—(4 miles to the inch, quarter sheets at 1s. 5d. each, whole sheets at 3s. 5d. each.) Various quarter-sheets and sheets added.

Miscellaneous.—Chih-li Province, China, 2 (2s. 8d.); China Field Force Survey, sheets 10, 11, 14, 2 (2s. 8d. each); Andaman Survey, sheets 1, 3–11, 2 (2s. 8d. each).

NOTE.—Particulars as to the above and lists of other publications on sale may be obtained on application to the Registrar and Superintendent of Records, India Office, or to any of the following—

AGENTS TO THE INDIA OFFICE FOR THE SALE OF GOVERNMENT PUBLICATIONS.

In London.

E. A. ARNOLD, 37, Bedford Street, Strand, W.C.

CONSTABLE and Co., 2, Whitehall Gardens, S.W.

P. S. KING and SON, 2 and 4, Great Smith Street, Westminster, S.W.

KEGAN PAUL, TRENCH, TRÜBNER and Co., 43, Gerrard Street, W.

B. QUARITCH, 15, Piccadilly, W.

In Oxford.

WILLIAMS and NORGATE, 7, Broad Street.

In Cambridge.

DEIGHTON, BELL and Co., Trinity Street.

On the Continent.

FRIEDLÄNDER und SOHN, 11, Carlstrasse, Berlin.

OTTO HARRASSOWITZ, Leipzig.

KARL W. HIERSEMANN, Leipzig.

ERNEST LEROUX, 28, Rue Bonaparte, Paris.

MARTINUS NIJHOFF, The Hague.

RECORD OF SERVICES.

(CORRECTED TO 1ST JULY, 1903.)

[The following List includes Officers of the Home Establishment of the Secretary of State, Officers of the Covenanted Civil Service, and those appointed under the Native Civil Service Rules, other Officers in Civil Employment in India whose SUBSTANTIVE pay is at least Rs. 1,000 a month, and, with a few exceptions, all Officers of those classes who have retired since 1886. It has not been found practicable to include more than a limited number of the Officers still surviving who retired before that year.]

EXPLANATION OF ABBREVIATIONS.
Acad.—Academy. Exhibr.—Exhibitioner. P.M.O. — Principal Medical Officer.
Acadl.—Academical. Expedn.—Expedition. Pol.—Political.
Acctnt.—Accountant. Expedny.—Expeditionary. Postmr.—Postmaster.
Accts.—Accounts. Fin.—Finance. Prest.—President.
Addl.—Additional. Finl.—Financial. Probr.—Probationer.
Addn.—Addition. Gen.—General. Proby.—Probationary.
Agric.—Agriculture. Govr.—Governor. Profr.—Professor.
Agricl.—Agricultural. Govt.—Government. Prov.—Provinces.
Apptd.—Appointed. Gr.—Grammar. Provl.—Provincial.
Asst.—Assistant. Hon.—Honorary. Publd.—Published.
Barr.—Barrister-at-Law. Hosp.—Hospital. Publn.—Publication.
Cantonmt.—Cantonment. Inspn.—Inspection. Registrn.—Registration.
Capt.—Captain. Inspr.—Inspector. Regt.—Regiment.
Col.—Colonel. Inst.—Institute. Rest.—Resident.
Coll.—College, Collegiate. Instn.—Institution. Retd.—Retired.
Collr.—Collector. Instrn.—Instruction. Rev.—Revenue.
Commn.—Commission. Irrign.—Irrigation. R.I.E. Coll.—Royal Indian Engineering College, Coopers Hill.
Commr.—Commissioner. Jud.—Judicial. Sch.—School.
Comptr.—Comptroller. Junr.—Junior. Sec.—Secretary.
Confd.—Confirmed. K.H.P.—King's Hon. Physician. Senr.—Senior.
Cov.—Covenanted. K.H.S.— „ „ Surgeon. Settlt.—Settlement.
Dep.—Deputy. K-i-H. medal.—Kaisar-i-Hind medal for public service in India. Subord.—Subordinate.
Depd.—Deputed. Legisl.—Legislative. Supt.—Superintendent.
Depn.—Deputation. Lient.—Lieutenant. Suptg.—Superintending.
Dept.—Department. Litho.—Lithographic. Surgn.—Surgeon.
Deptl.—Departmental. Mag.—Magistrate. Topogr.—Topographical.
Dir.—Director. Med.—Medical. Transfd.—Transferred.
Divl.—Divisional. Memo.—Memorandum. Transld.—Translated.
Divn.—Division. Mil.—Military. Transln.—Translation.
Ecclesl.—Ecclesiastical. Offd.—Officiated. Translr.—Translator.

Examr.—Examiner.

RECORD OF SERVICES.

ABBOTT, EVELYN ROBINS, Indian C.S. (asst. commr., Punjab).—Educ. at Bath Coll., and Balliol Coll., Oxford; apptd. after exam. of 1891; arrived, 30th Oct., 1893, and served in the Punjab as asst. commr.

ABBOTT, HENRY BYAM, Lieut.-Col., I.S.C., late Foreign Dept., Govt. of India.—First commn., June, 1861; asst. rest., Aden, 1865; from Oct., 1869, served as asst. agent, Rajputana; pol. asst., Manपुरa, and dep. Bhil agent, 1875-76; supt. of Jhalawar, March, 1876; pol. agent, Alwar, Jan., 1891; rest., W. states of Rajputana, April, 1892; offg. govr.-gen. agent in Rajputana, March, 1895; returned to Europe, 1898.

ABBOTT, HERBERT EDWARD STACY, D.S.O., Lieut.-Col., R.E., India Public Works Dept. (suptg. engr.).—Joined the service, 16th Sept., 1878, and was posted to the Punjab; on field service during Afghan war, 1880-81 (medal); exec. engr., Jan., 1885; field engr. in Hazara expedn., 1888 (medal and clasp), and in Miranzai expedn., 1891 (clasp); with Chitral force, March, 1895; under sec. to Punjab govt., public works dept., May, 1893, and again Sept., 1895; D.S.O., Jan., 1895; suptg. engr., Aug., 1902.

ABDUL GHAUR KHAN OF ZAIDA, Khan Bahadur (dist. judge, Punjab).—Apptd. prob. under native civil service rules, 14th Feb., 1889; asst. commr., Dec., 1891; dist. judge, Aug., 1898.

ABDULLAH IBN YUSUF ALI, Khan Bahadur, M.A., LL.M., Barr. (1896), Indian C.S. (asst. mag. and collr., United Prov.).—Educ. at Bombay Univ. and St. John's Coll., Camb., and Lincoln's Inn, London; apptd. after exam. of 1894; arrived, 18th Jan., 1896, and served in N.W.P. and Oudh as asst. mag. and collr.; asst. sessions judge, March, 1902; author of a monograph on silk fabrics.

ABDULLATIF QAMR-UD-DIN AMIR-UD-DIN LATIF, B.A., LL.B. Barr. (1902), Indian C.S. (asst. commr. Punjab).—Educ. at St. Xavier's Sch., Bombay, Bombay Univ., and St. John's Coll., Cambridge; apptd. after exam. of 1901; arrived, 8th Jan., 1903, and served in the Punjab as asst. commr.

ABDUR RAHIM, B.L. (presidency mag., Calcutta).—Apptd. presidency mag., Calcutta, 18th March, 1901.

ABDUR RAHMAN, A. F. M., Khan Bahadur, Barr. (1880), (judge, Calcutta small cause court).—Apptd. offg. judge, Calcutta small cause court, Oct., 1895; confd., Jan., 1897.

ABRAHAMS, LIONEL, B.A., India Office (sec., Finl. Dept.).—Educ. at the City of London Sch. and Balliol Coll., Oxford (Arnold prize, 1894); junr. clerk, 21st Feb., 1893; senior clerk, April, 1900; précis writer, Dec., 1894; joint editor, India Office List, Nov., 1896, to April, 1900; asst. sec., Indian Currency committee, 1898; asst. sec., finl. dept., Oct., 1901; sec., finl. dept., July, 1902.

ABUD, HENRY MALLABY, Lieut.-Col., Indian Army, Pol. Dept., Bombay (asst. pol. rest., Aden).—First commn., 27th Jan., 1876; in mil. employ till April, 1886, when he was apptd. to the Kolhapur Inf. Corps, and asst. to pol. agent,

Kolhapur; asst. to pol. rest., Aden, Nov., 1886; asst. to pol. supt., Savantvadi, Nov., 1896; asst. pol. agent, Kathiawar, Mar., 1898; ditto, Savantvadi, April, 1899; asst. to pol. rest., Aden, Nov. 1899; served with Aden Boundary Commn. from Jan. to July, 1902; acting pol. rest., Aden, July to Sept., 1902.

ABUL HASSAN, Barr. (1880), (judge, Calcutta small cause court).—Joined the service Dec., 1887, as registrar, Calcutta small cause court; offd. as judge, small cause court, May, 1891; confd., Oct., 1895.

ABU TURAB KHAN, Agha, Statutory C.S. (asst. commr., United Prov.).—Apptd. prob. asst. mag. and collr. in N.W. Prov. and Oudh, under the statutory civil service rules, 16th April, 1889; confd., 8th Aug., 1892; joint mag., Feb., 1897; asst. commr., Dec. 1901.

ACKLOM, ROBERT EVATT (late Police Dept., Cent. Provs.).—Joined the service, 18th April, 1867, as asst. district supt. of police; supt., Rajputana-Malwa railway police, May, 1883; insp.-gen., Aug., 1893; insp.-gen. of police and jails, Cent. Prov., April, 1897; ret'd., May, 1901.

ACWORTH, HARRY ARBUTHNOT, C.I.E., Barr. (1870), late Indian C.S. (Bombay).—Educ. at Brighton Coll.; apptd. after exam. of 1868; arrived, 31st Oct., 1870, and served as asst. collr. and mag., dep. municipal commr., and dep. collr. of salt rev.; member of mixed commn. at Goa, 1884-85; municipal commr. for city of Bombay, April, 1890; C.I.E., May 1895; editor of the Bombay Rev. Handbook, 3rd edn., and author of 'Ballads of the Marathas,' 1894; ret'd., March, 1896.

ADAM, JAMES, A.M.I.C.E., F.C.H., India Public Works Dept. (exec. engr.).—Trained at R.I.E. Coll.; apptd. asst. engr., 23rd Sept., 1885, and posted to N.W. Prov. and Oudh; transferred to State Rys., April, 1887; exec. engr., Aug., 1895; employed in Kashmir state, 1899-1901; dep. consg. engr. for rlws. Bombay, Oct., 1901; ditto, and under sec., Feb. 1902.

ADAMI, LEONARD CHRISTIAN, Indian C.S. (asst. mag. and collr., Bengal).—Educ. at Cheltenham and Jesus Coll., Cambridge; apptd. after exam. of 1896; arrived, 9th Dec., 1897, and served in Bengal as asst. mag. and collr.

ADAMS, GEORGE, late Indian C.S. (N.W. Prov. and Oudh).—Educ. at Christ's Hosp.; apptd. after exam. of 1860; arrived, 30th Dec., 1861, and served in the N.W. Prov. as asst. mag. and collr., dep. commr., and mag. and collr.; commr. in Oudh, Nov., 1889; commr., Benares, Aug., 1890; Lucknow, April, 1893; junr. member, board of rev., April, 1894; also member of provl. legisl. council, June, 1894; ret'd., Dec., 1896.

ADAMS, GEORGE FREDERICK, B.A., Indian C.S. (asst. mag. and collr., United Prov.).—Educ. at Otago Univ., N.Z., and Wadham Coll., Oxford; apptd. after exam. of 1898; arrived 1st Dec., 1899, and served in the N.W. Prov. and Oudh as asst. mag. and collr.

ADAMS, JOHN, late Bombay Public Works Dept.—Joined the service, 13th May, 1869; asst. engr., Nov., 1871; exec. engr., Sept.,

1881; architectural exec. engr. and surveyor, Jan., 1882; is a fellow of Bombay univ.; ret'd., Sept., 1900.

ADAMSON, CHARLES HENRY ELLISON, C.I.E., Lieut.-Col., I.S.C., late Burma Comm.,—First commn., royal artillery, Jan., 1868; joined the Burma commn., Dec., 1871, and served as asst. mag., asst. commr., and prest. of municipal committee, Rangoon; dep. commr., June, 1885; C.I.E., May, 1889; commr., Jan., 1892; ret'd., Oct., 1896.

ADAMSON, HARVEY, C.S.I., M.A., Indian C.S. (Jud. commr., Upper Burma).—Educ. at the Gymnasium, Old Aberdeen, and King's Coll., Aberdeen; apptd. after exam. of 1875; arrived, 17th Dec., 1877, and served in Burma as asst. commr. and settlt. officer; dep. commr., April, 1888; commr., July, 1898; jud. commr. Upper Burma, April, 1902; C.S.I., Jan., 1903; addl. member of viceroy's legisl. council, Nov. 1903.

ADDIS, DAVID FOULIS, M.A., late Indian C.S. (N.W. Prov. and Oudh).—Educ. at the Univs. of Glasgow and Edinburgh; apptd. after exam. of 1870; arrived, 14th Nov., 1872, and served in the N.W. Prov. and Oudh as asst. mag. and collr., joint mag., asst. settlt. officer, and dep. commr.; mag. and collr., July, 1890; district and sessions judge, March, 1895; ret'd., Oct., 1897.

ADDIS, HENRY BAWN, late India Public Works Dept.—Joined the service, 25th Jan., 1871, as asst. engr., and was employed on the Nimach, W. Rajputana and other railways; exec. engr., April, 1878; ret'd., Aug., 1894.

ADIE, ALFRED EDMOND, M.I.C.E., Indian Public Works Dept. (traffic supt.).—Trained at the Thomason Coll., Roorkee; apptd. apprentice engineer, March, 1874, and posted to state railways; traffic supt., Feb., 1880.

ADIE, WALTER SIBBARD, B.A., Indian C.S. (dep. accont.-gen., Madras).—Educ. at Trin. Coll., Camb., apptd. after exam. of 1895; arrived, 7th Dec., 1896, and served in Bengal as asst. mag. and collr.; asst. comptr., Assam, Sept., 1899; asst. accont.-gen., Burma, April, 1900; dep. accont.-gen., Madras, Dec., 1901.

ADINARAYANA AIYA, M., Rao Bahadur, late settlt. dept., Madras.—Accnt., rev. board office, Madras, Feb., 1867; registrar, rev. settlt. dept., Oct., 1887; asst. commr., Feb., 1889; dep. commr., Dec., 1894; ret'd., March, 1902.

AFZAL KHAN, M. (see Muhammad Afzal Khan).

AGAR, AGAR PADLEY (dist. supt. of Police, Madras).—Joined the service, 18th Nov., 1876; asst. supt., Aug., 1877; supt., April, 1886.

AGASTI, S. K. (see Surja Kumar Agasti).

AGNEW, PATRICK DALREAGLE, Indian C.S. (dep. commr., Punjab).—Educ. at Bedford Gr. Sch. and Balliol Coll., Oxford; apptd. after exam. of 1887; arrived, 1st Dec., 1889, and served in the Punjab as asst. commr. and asst. settlt. officer; dep. commr., Dec., 1898.

AHMADI, I.S.D. (see Ibrahim Shaik Daud Ahmadi).

AHMED, D. H. (see Delawar Hosain Ahmed).
AHSANUDDIN AHMAD, Barr. (1881), Statutory C.S. (mag. and collr., Bengal).—Apptd. asst. mag. and collr. under the native civil service rules, 3rd Jan., 1882; confd., June, 1887; joint mag. and dep. collr., March, 1893; mag. and collr., Dec., 1899.

AIKMAN, ROBERT SMITH, M.A., Indian C.S. (judge, high court, N.W. Prov.).—Educ. at

Madras Coll., St. Andrew's, and St. Andrew's Univ.; apptd. after exam. of 1865; arrived, 22nd Nov., 1867, and served in the N.W. Prov. as asst. mag. and collr., and joint mag. and dep. collr.; on famine relief duty in Bengal, 1874; from March, 1884, served in the jud. dept.; puisne judge, high court, Sept., 1896; vice-chancellor, Allahabad univ., Jan., 1898.

AINSLIE, WILLIAM, late Bengal C.S.—Apptd. from Haileybury Coll., 1845; arrived, 7th Nov., 1845; served in Bengal as asst. and joint mag. and dep. collr. in Cuttack; specially employed in 1850-51 and 1851-52 in the suppression of Meriah sacrifices in the Kandh districts; mag. of Rangpur, July, 1852; railway commr., E. Indian railway, 1856-57; mag. and collr., Bhagalpur, March, 1857; railway commr., E. Bengal railway, 1858, and subsequently of the S.E. railway also; from Aug., 1863, served in the jud. dept., and was apptd. civil and sessions judge of Patna, Jan., 1864; prest., tea commn., 1867-68; judge of the high court, Calcutta, May, 1873; ret'd., April, 1880.

AITKEN, EDWARD HAMILTON, B.A., Salt Dept., Bombay (asst. collr.).—Joined the service 19th April, 1876, as asst. commr. of customs; asst. collr. of salt rev., July, 1881.

AIYA, A. (see Adinarayana Aiya).

AIYANGAR, P. (see Parthasarathi Aiyangar).

AIYANGAR, S. S. R. (see Srinavasa Raghava Aiyangar).

AIYANGAR, SIR V. B. (see Bhashyam Aiyangar, Sir V.).

AIYAR, G. C. (see Gopala Krishna Aiyar).

AIYAR, R. (see Ramachandra Aiyar).

AIYAR, R. N. (see Narayana Aiyar R.).

AIYAR, R. V. S. (see Srinivasa Aiyar, R. V.).

AIYAR, SIR S. (see Subramaniya Aiyar).

AIYAR, T. M. A. S. (see Swaminatha Aiyar, T. M. A.).

AKBAR, A. (see Ali Akbar).

AKBAR HUSAIN, Saiyid, Khan Bahadur, late Jud. Dept., United Prov.—Joined the service, 25th Nov., 1880, as munsif; sub. judge, Aug., 1888; judge of small cause courts, March, 1894; ret'd., Dec., 1903; has translated the Legal Remembrancer's series into Urdu.

ALBION RAJ KUMAR BANERJI (see Banerji, A. R. K.).

ALCOCK, ALFRED WILLIAM, C.I.E., M.B., Major, Indian Med. Service (supt. Indian Museum, and prof'r., Medical Coll., Calcutta).—Educ. at Westminster Sch. and Aberdeen Univ.; first commn., 1st Oct., 1885; in mil. employ till Nov., 1888, when he was apptd. surgn.-naturalist, marine survey dept.; dep. sany. commr., Bengal, Sept. 1892; supt. Indian Museum, and prof'r. of Zoology, etc., Med. Coll., Calcutta, May, 1893; C.I.E., Jan., 1903; author of numerous scientific papers.

ALCOCK, JOHN BACKHOUSE, late Bombay C.S.—Apptd. after exam. of 1874; arrived, 10th Nov., 1876, and served as asst. collr. and mag., and railway mag.; from Jan., 1884, served as asst. and joint judge and sessions judge; judge and sessions judge, March, 1894; ret'd., Nov., 1901.

ALCOCK, WILFRID OWEN, Indian C.S. (asst. collr. and mag., Bombay).—Educ. at Blundell's Sch., Tiverton, and Balliol Coll., Oxford; apptd. after exam. of 1897; arrived, 3rd Dec., 1898, and served in Bombay as asst. collr. and mag.

ALDER, WILFRED, B.A., Indian C.S. (asst. commr., United Prov.).—Educ. at Trinity College, Cambridge; apptd. after exam. of 1899; arrived, 26th Nov., 1900, and served in the N.W. Prov. and Oudh, as asst. commr., and asst. mag. and collr.

ALEXANDER, CHARLES LESLIE, M.A., Indian C.S. (asst. mag. and collr., United Prov.).—Educ. at Shrewsbury and Trin. Coll., Cambridge; apptd. after exam. of 1897; arrived, 7th Dec., 1898, and served in the N.W. Prov. as asst. mag. and collr.

ALEXANDER, EDWIN JOHN, late India Public Works Dept. —Trained at R.I.E. Coll.; apptd. to the Dept. as assist. engr., Sept., 1880, and posted to State Railways; exec. engr., Aug., 1893; ret'd., April, 1903.

ALEXANDER, ERNEST BRUCE, late Indian C.S. (United Prov.).—Educ. at Repton; apptd. after exam. of 1869; arrived, 1st Nov., 1871, and served in the N.W. Prov. and Oudh as asst. mag. and collr., asst. settlt. officer, junr. sec. to the board of rev., joint mag. and dep. collr., and dep. commr.; mag. and collr., Oct., 1889; in 1894 acted as sec. to govt., jud. dept.; commr., Nov., 1896; ret'd., Sept., 1903; author of 'Gazetteer of Gorakhpur.'

ALEXANDER, HENRY NATHANIEL, Jail Dept., Bombay (inspr.-gen. of prisons).—Entered the service, 26th March, 1872, and served in the Indo-Euro. tel. dep.; assist. supt. of police, Sept., 1878; supt., Nov., 1884; inspr.-gen. of prisons, April, 1901.

ALEXANDER, JAMES WILLIAM, late India Public Works Dept. —Joined the service, June, 1865; asst. engr., Dec., 1866; asst. sec. to govt. of India, public works dept., Jan., 1868; exec. engr., March, 1870; suptg. engr., Feb., 1890; ret'd., Mar., 1899.

ALEXANDER, MOSES, India Office (Second class clerk, Store Dept.).—Clerk, 2nd class, 3rd March, 1888.

ALEXANDER, NATHANIEL STUART, late Bengal C.S.—Apptd. from Haileybury Coll.; arrived, 10th July, 1858; from Oct., 1859, served in the Bengal rev. and jud. depts., and was apptd. mag. and collr., 1st grade, Jan., 1879; commr., Dacca, Aug., 1884; Bhagalpur, Dec., 1885; and Bardwan, Aug., 1887; ret'd., March, 1890.

ALGIE, WILLIAM, B.A., B.C.E., India Public Works Dept. (exec. engr.).—Apptd. apprentice engr., 19th April, 1875, and posted to Burma; asst. engr., April, 1876; exec. engr., May, 1885; employed on irrign. works in Egypt, 1885-86.

ALI, A. (see Ameer Ali).

ALI, A. (see Asghar Ali).

ALI AHMED HUSSANALLY, Barr. (1893), Indian C.S. (asst. mag. and collr., United Prov.).—Educ. at Karachi Mohammedan Sch., Bombay Univ., and Queen's Coll., Oxford; apptd. after exam. of 1894; arrived 27th Feb., 1896, and served in the N.W. Prov. and Oudh as asst. mag. and collr. and asst. commr.

ALI AKBAR, A.M.I.C.E., Bombay Public Works Dept. (exec. engr.).—Trained at R.I.E. Coll.; joined the service, 1st Oct., 1882, as asst. engr.; exec. engr., June, 1896.

ALI, I. (see Inam Ali).

ALI KHAN, G. (see Ghazanfur Ali Khan).

ALI, M. (see Muhammad Ali).

ALISON, SIR ARCHIBALD, Bart., G.C.B., Gen., late member of Council of India.—

First commn., ensign, 72nd foot, 3rd Nov., 1846; served in the Crimean campaign of 1855, and was present at siege of Sevastopol (medal and clasp and Turkish medal); served in the Indian Mutiny, and was mil. sec. to the commander-in-chief, Aug., 1857, to March, 1858; was present at relief of Lucknow (arm lost, medal and clasp, and C.B.); in staff employment, adjutant-gen.'s dept. at headquarters, and in the south western district, and at Aldershot, 1862-73; served in the Ashanti war of 1873-74 as brigadier-gen. of European brigade (thanks of both houses of parliament, medal with clasp, and K.C.B.); dep. adjutant-gen., headquarters, Ireland, 1874-77; commandant, staff college, 1878; dep. quartermaster-gen., intelligence branch, 1878-82; served in the Egyptian expdn. of 1882 as major-gen. in command of troops at Alexandria, and on the Mahmoudiyeh canal, and in command of 3rd brigade, 2nd divn., at battle of Tel-el-Kebir (thanks of both houses of parliament, medal with clasp, bronze star, and 2nd class Osmanieh); promoted lieut.-gen. for distinguished service, Nov., 1882; commanding divn., Aldershot, Aug., 1883, to Feb., 1885; and again, Oct., 1885; adjutant-gen. to the forces, Feb.-Sept., 1885; G.C.B., June, 1887; member of the council of India, Jan., 1889, to Jan., 1899.

ALLANSON, HARRY LLEWELYAN LYONS, Indian C.S. (asst. mag. and collr. Bengal).—Educ. at Bedford Grammar School and Exeter Coll., Oxford; apptd. after exam. of 1899; arrived, 13 Dec., 1900, and served in Bengal as asst. mag. and collr.

ALLEN, BASIL CPLESTON, B.A., Indian C.S. (asst. commr., Assam).—Educ. at Haileybury and Corpus Christi Coll., Oxford; apptd. after exam. of 1892; arrived, 14th Dec., 1893, and served in Assam as asst. commr. and personal asst. to chief commr.; offg. dir. of land records and agric., May to Nov., 1902.

ALLEN, CHARLES FRANCIS EGERTON, B.A., Barr. (1871), late Jud. Dept., Burma.—Called to the bar (Inner Temple), 1871; admitted advocate of high court, Calcutta, Nov., 1873; law examr., Calcutta univ., Dec., 1874, and again in 1875 and 1876; acting judge of the Calcutta court of small causes, 1875-76; lecturer on English law, presidency coll., Calcutta, Feb., 1876, and profr. of law (temporary), May, 1877; served as govt. advocate, Rangoon, and acted as recorder of Rangoon for various periods; M.P. for Pembroke district, 1892-95.

ALLEN, CHARLES GEORGE HILLERSDEN, Indian C.S. (mag. and collr.
